trajectory_summary: The call moved Joshua from passive compliance to a booked appointment, but the booking is structurally fragile. Nefer never addressed Joshua's stated product expectation (Tesla solar roof tiles), never flipped the $250 bill anchor emotionally, and skipped the button-up elements that protect sit rate. The energy never built — it stayed flat from open to close.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked appointment, so a 'booked' tag would be technically accurate. However, the appointment is fragile: Joshua's primary interest is in a product Ion does not sell (Tesla solar roof tiles), and the closer will walk into an expectation gap that was never reframed. A 'booked' tag without a fragility flag understates the risk.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Tesla expectation gap reframe + roofing cross-sell bridge
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Oh, I was at I was exploring the solar roofs, so I don't know what that looks like. It looks like they have their own shingles that are solar panels, and then they they have, like, a hub that they store energy in.
primary_coaching_focus.why: These two misses are connected and compound each other. Joshua's primary stated interest is a product Ion doesn't sell (Tesla solar roof tiles), and his primary stated need is a new roof — which Ion does sell. Nefer addressed neither. The closer will open the proposal call against a customer who expects a product that isn't on the menu, with no roofing conversation started. A 15-second reframe at 01:02 and a roofing bridge at 00:40 would have protected the sit and potentially captured a roofing lead. The manager should work both moves in the same coaching session — they're triggered by the same customer disclosure.

key_moments.quote: Oh, I was at I was exploring the solar roofs, so I don't know what that looks like. It looks like they have their own shingles that are solar panels, and then they they have, like, a hub that they store energy in.
key_moments.stewart_read: Joshua is describing Tesla solar roof tiles — the integrated shingle product Ion explicitly does not sell. This is the expectation gap the schema flags as real but narrow. Nefer's response ('Exactly. You're you're kinda just, doing your research. Okay. Yeah. And, I definitely love to help out there.') validates his research without reframing what Ion actually offers. The closer will open the proposal call against a customer who believes he's getting a product that isn't on the menu. Strategic read: Nefer may have been deferring the product explanation to the closer. Reactive read: Nefer didn't recognize the gap at all. The absence of any reframe — even a soft one — supports the reactive read.

key_moments.quote: I'm gonna need a new roof. So
key_moments.stewart_read: Joshua's first substantive answer is that he needs a new roof. This is a direct trigger for the roofing cross-sell per the schema. Nefer's response ('Oh, okay. I wanna see what this looks like. Mhmm. It's fine. Yeah. Awesome. Okay. We can can definitely help you out there.') acknowledges the roof need but does not bridge to Ion's in-house roofing capability. The schema is explicit: 'Solar disqualified by roof timing = potential roof sale + future solar sale. ALWAYS BRIDGE.' Nefer's trailing 'It's fine' suggests she was processing internally but no strategic pivot was executed.

A $250/month bill is strong pain. Nefer captures it ('Oh, jeez. Okay. We can definitely help out there.') and moves on. The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — Nefer executes ask + acknowledge and drops the flip. The bill is never turned into emotional alignment against Dominion. This is the universal floor pattern the schema flags: 'Across all 7 deep-read calls, every rep captured the bill anchor and NONE flipped it.'

key_moments.quote: yeah, that should be found on any recent Dominion bill in the form of a bar graph. And so do you know if you get those online or in the mail?
key_moments.stewart_read: Nefer initiates the 12-month bill collection path before establishing how long Joshua has lived at the home. The residence length question ('How long have they lived at this home?') is a verify item that, if asked first, would have surfaced the new-homeowner status immediately and avoided the bill-path dead end. Instead, Nefer asks about bill format, Joshua reveals he just bought the house on April 16, and the path collapses. The sequencing gap is distinct from the new-homeowner pivot that follows — it's a procedural ordering miss that created unnecessary friction.
